MLO1: Demonstrate a critical understanding of the technical methods used to conduct an AI or data science investigation. (K3, K7, K18, K19, K25)

MLO2: Critically assess applications of adequate techniques for understanding and exploring business data and how areas can be exploited for critical decision-making through the engagement in statistical programming frameworks. (K22, K27)

MLO3: Appraise and apply different AI techniques to realistic business environments which deal with huge volumes of data and exploit this through programming-based frameworks. (B7, S2, S11, S14, S20, S25)

MLO4: Reflect on solving realistic business environment problems using suitably applied statistical techniques. (S2, K26)

MLO5: Build a critical awareness of legal, cultural, and ethical issues that surround data analysis, including the exploration and dissemination of data. (B3)

Knowledge 

- K3: How to apply advanced statistical and mathematical methods to commercial projects. 
- K7: How to solve problems and evaluate software solutions via analysis of test data and results from research, feasibility, acceptance, and usability testing. 
- K18: Programming language and techniques applicable for data engineering. 
- K19: Principles and properties behind statistical and machine learning methods. 
- K22: The relationship between mathematical principles and core techniques in AI and data science within organisational context. 
- K23: Use different performance and accuracy metrics for model validation in AI projects. 
- K25: Programming languages and modern machine learning libraries for commercially beneficial scientific analysis and solutions. 
- K26: Scientific methods and their applications in research and business context, including experimental design and hypothesis testing. 
- K27: The engineering principles used (general and software) to create new instruments and applications for data collection. 

Skills

- S2: Independently analyse test data, interpret results, and evaluate the suitability of proposed solutions, considering current and future business requirements. 
- S11: Apply aspects of advanced maths and statistics relevant to AI and data science that delivers business outcomes. 
- S14: Work collaboratively with software engineers to ensure suitable testing and documentation processes are implemented. 
- S20: Design efficient algorithms for accessing and analysing large amounts of data, including Application Programming Interfaces (API) to different databases and datasets. 
- S25: Select and use programming languages and tools, following appropriate software development practices.

Behaviours 

- B3: Acts with integrity with respect to ethical, legal, and regulatory factors, ensuring the protection of personal data, safety, and security. 
- B7: Participates and shares best practice in their organisation and the wider community around all aspects of AI and data science.

Recommended reading list for Programming for Artificial Intelligence

The main texts for this module are:

[VanderPlas, J., Python data science handbook](https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook/) | github.io |

[Deisenroth, M.P., Faisal, A.A. and Ong, C.S. (2020) Mathematics for machine learning](https://mml-book.github.io/) | [github.io](https://github.io/) |

[Shafer and Zhang (2022) Introductory Statistics](https://stats.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Introductory_Statistics/Introductory_Statistics_\(Shafer_and_Zhang\)) | LibreTexts |

[McKinney, W. (2017) Python for data analysis: data wrangling with Pandas, NumPy, and IPython](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1449444) | VLeBooks |

[VanderPlas, J. (2016) A whirlwind tour of Python](https://jakevdp.github.io/WhirlwindTourOfPython/) | [github.io](https://github.io/) |

[Pears, R. and Shields, G.J. (2019) Cite them right: the essential referencing guide](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/2025007)| VLeBooks |

[Kelleher, J.D. and Tierney, B. (2018) Data science](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/2061950) | VLeBooks |

[Sarkar, D. (2019) Text analytics with Python: a practitioner’s guide to natural language processing](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1888038) | VLeBooks |

[https://app.qa.com/download/dam/7551f4de-8fd3-4403-9e5e-6abb1ce2af51](https://app.qa.com/download/dam/7551f4de-8fd3-4403-9e5e-6abb1ce2af51)

# Main

## Machine Learning

Types

1. Supervised - Labelled dataset
2. Unsupervised - Finds patterns, no labels
3. Reinforcement - algorithm acts as an agent, learns through trail and error using feedback from actions.
4. Semi-supervised - combines supervised and unsupervised learning. E.g. training a model on a small dataset, then using the high confidence predictions to train a model on more data.

BCS use

1. Supervised
2. Unsupervised
3. Semi-supervised
4. Deep learning

## Machine learning hypothesis

Could be that a model will map inputs to outputs.

Hypothesises

- Scientific hypothesis - explanation for observations that are falsifiable.
- Statistical hypothesis - explanation about the relationship between data samples that is interpreted probabilistically.
- Machine learning hypothesis - is a model that approximates a target function for mapping inputs to outputs.

A Machine learning hypothesis is a bit like a scientific one, in that it's falsifiable.

## The Machine learning pipeline

- Data preparation
- Model training
- Model optimisation
- Model evaluation
- Deployment

![Model 
maintainance 
Data 
Model 
Model 
Model 
preparation 
training 
optimisation 
evaluation 
Deployment](data:image/png;base64,)

### Data Preparation

Collect, clean, transforming and labelling data.

Often it's that first part that is hard, getting access to the actual data or system.

Data pre can take 80% of the time.

- Correcting error and standardising representations
- Remove unwanted field
- Handling missing values
- Handling outliers
- Normalisation and standardisation - normalisation is moving values to a range e.g. [0,1], standardisation is giving it a standard distribution (x-mean)/sd
- Organising the data for machine learning
- Model Training
- Cross- validation

## Data preparation

Cleaning includes

- Removing unwated fields,
- correcting errors,
- standardising representations,
- Handling missing values,
- handing outliers
- Normalisation and standardisation
- Organise data for ME, what are features, labels, test/train split, feature engineering
- Model training
- Cross-validation

### Removing unwanted fields

Require domain knowledge, but could also benefit from PCA

### Missing date

1. Missing completely at random(MCAR) No pattern
2. Missing at random(MAR) e.g. test scores could be taken by a person, but we don’t
3. Missing not at random(MNAR) e.g. overweight people might have been advised to not put in their weight.

Can impute missing values

Multivariate imputed by chained equations(Mice)

Domain knowledge is required for outriggers, and there need to be enough to warrant attention.

### Normalisation and standardisation

![Xi - M 
= Z](data:image/png;base64,)

### Feature engineering

Selecting manipulating and transforming raw data int features that can be used in learning.

PCA can help determine the best features.

Could combine values, e.g. BMI works better than weight or height alone. Would a simple LLM create a BMI scale?

If that has categorical encoding use one hot encoding.

[https://machinelearningmastery.com/why-one-hot-encode-data-in-machine-learning/](https://machinelearningmastery.com/why-one-hot-encode-data-in-machine-learning/) TO REad

Split data into

Trans, validate , test

Hyper-parameter tuning

- Proportion of data in train, test
- Learning rate, batch size. Number od epochs.

The training data is used for training

The validation data is used for tuning

The test data is unseen data only used for evaluation

### Cross-Validation

Splits out different different train and validation sets but more dynamically.

### Evaluation

Classification - prediction the class of an item

Regression - Model that represents data item.

### Evaluation metrics

Regression

- Mean Square Error(MSE)
- Mean Absolute Error(MAE)
- Root Mean Square Error(RMSE)
- R^2(Coefficient of determination)

The error is the quantification of the distance between the real and predicted values.

Classification

- Accuracy
- Precision
- Recall
- F1

Confusion matrix

Receiver Operator Characteristic(ROC) curve

![Actually 
Actually 
positive (1) 
negative (0) 
Predicted 
True 
False 
positive (1) 
positives 
positives 
(TPs) 
(FPs) 
Predicted 
False 
True 
negative (0) 
negatives 
negatives 
(FNs) 
(TNs)](data:image/png;base64,)

![Perfect 
classifier 
ROC curve 
1.0 
Better 
0.5 
Worse 
Random classifier 
True positive rate 
0.0 
0.0 
0.5 
1.0 
False positive rate](data:image/png;base64,)

Precision = TP/(TP + FP)

Recall = TP(TP + FN)

F2 =  1 * R * P/(P +R)

Accuracy = TP + TN/(TP +TN + FP +TN)

For imbalanced datasets a high accuracy score could be misleading.

There are balanced accuracies.

Synthetic Minority Oversamples Techniques(SMOTE) is a method to handle imbalances.

Journal

- S13: Identify appropriate resources and architectures for solving a computational problem within the workplace. 
- S14: Work collaboratively with software engineers to ensure suitable testing and documentation processes are implemented.

Hubble need to integrate into other systems. Should speak to Illuminate team.

### Regression

Capture relationships.

Common one is linear regerssion.

from sklearn import linear_model  
mymodel = linear_model. LinearRegression( )  
mymodel.fit (X_train, y_train)

Bias-Variance Trade-Off in Machine Learning

What is Bias and Variance?

- Bias in ML is the error due to overly simplistic assumptions made by the model to approximate the true relationship between input and output. High bias means the model misses relevant relations (underfitting).
- Variance is the error due to the model being too sensitive to fluctuations in the training data, learning noise instead of the underlying pattern (overfitting).

What Increases Bias?

- Using too simple models (e.g., linear when data is nonlinear).
- Underfitting the training data (insufficient model complexity).
- Too much regularization pushing model parameters toward zero.

What Increases Variance?

- Highly complex models with many parameters.
- Overfitting training data by capturing noise.
- Small training data sets or noisy data.

Model Types by Bias and Variance:

- High Bias, Low Variance: Simple models like linear regression tend to consistently miss patterns.
- Low Bias, High Variance: Complex models like decision trees capture training details tightly but fail to generalize well.

Preference Between Bias and Variance

The ideal model has low bias and low variance, but this is practically unattainable. We instead aim for a trade-off that minimizes total error. High bias and high variance is worse, as it indicates poor fit and inconsistency.

Methods to Decrease Variance

- Increase training data size.
- Use regularization (L1/L2 penalties).
- Employ ensemble methods (bagging, random forests).
- Prune decision trees.
- Use cross-validation.

Regression Techniques Tackling Bias-Variance

- Ridge regression (L2) and Lasso regression (L1) add constraints to reduce variance by penalizing complexity, increasing bias slightly but improving generalization.

Example Model Bias and Variance

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|Model Type|Bias|Variance|
|Linear Regression|High Bias|Low Variance|
|Decision Tree|Low Bias|High Variance|
|Random Forest|Low Bias|Moderate Variance|

Visual Summary

Imagine the classic illustration of bias and variance:

- High bias: darts consistently off-center (systematically wrong).
- High variance: darts widely scattered (inconsistent).
- Low bias and low variance: darts tightly clustered at the bullseye (accurate and precise).
- Prog

Error in book,s should be Change 4 ** x ** 2 to 4 * x ** 2 in your code.

The set text for this module is:

[Vanderplas, J. Python Data Science Handbook, O'Reilly](https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook) | github.io |

The text concentrates on the programming side of things whereas the lessons here on the QA platform focus on more theoretical aspects. To enhance your programming skills, you should work through the textbook at your own pace. Sometimes chapters or sections will be pointed out for you to read.

During the first two weeks of the module, you should read chapters 1 and 2.

Try the coding examples shown as you read through. For some of you, the contents might already be known, in which case just skim through or skip those parts.

Bibliography

The bibliography is the list of articles consulted while authoring these notes and includes references. It is not expected for you to read all these articles, but please do dip into those you find interesting, or think might be helpful.

[Akosa, J. (2017) "Predictive Accuracy: A Misleading Performance Measure for Highly Imbalanced Data" SAS Papers](https://support.sas.com/resources/papers/proceedings17/0942-2017.pdf) (accessed 20-09-24)

[Brownlee, J. (2020a) "What is a Hypothesis in Machine Learning?", Machine Learning Mastery, Sept. 4](https://machinelearningmastery.com/what-is-a-hypothesis-in-machine-learning/) (accessed 20-09-24)

[Brownlee, J. (2020b) "Iterative Imputation for Missing Values in Machine Learning", Machine Learning Mastery, August 18](https://machinelearningmastery.com/iterative-imputation-for-missing-values-in-machine-learning/) (accessed 20-09-24)

[Brownlee, J. (2021) "SMOTE for Imbalanced Classification with Python", Machine Learning Mastery, Sept. 4](https://machinelearningmastery.com/smote-oversampling-for-imbalanced-classification/) (accessed 20-09-24)

[Chan, C (n.d.) "What is a ROC Curve and How to Interpret It", DisplayR](https://www.displayr.com/what-is-a-roc-curve-how-to-interpret-it/)(accessed 20-09-24)

[Joleen (2023) "Evaluating Classification Models: Understanding the Confusion Matrix and ROC Curves", Data Science Tutorials](https://statisticallyrelevant.com/confusion-matrix-and-roc-curves/#:~:text=The%20confusion%20matrix%20breaks%20down,apart%20at%20different%20decision%20thresholds.) (accessed 20-09-24)

[Varsity Tutors (n.d.) "Line of Best Fit". Hotmath Topics](https://www.varsitytutors.com/hotmath/hotmath_help/topics/line-of-best-fit) (accessed 20-09-24)
